CALIFORNIA
POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
925 L Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

December 3, 1975

Mr. John F. Henning
Executive Secretary-Treasurer
California Labor Federation
995 Market Street
San Francisco, Ca. 94103

Re: Opinion Request
No. 75-176

Dear Mr. Henning:

At its meeting on December 2, 1975, the Commission con-
sidered its previous response to your request for an opinion,
No. 75-176, in light of the decision by the Los Angeles County
Superior Court in Institute of Governmental Advocates v.
Younger, No. C 110,052, and authorized me to advise you of the
following:

(1) Although the Commission continues to believe that
the views expressed in my letter of October 27, 1975, represent
a correct interpretation of the law, the Commission intends to
comply with the preliminary injunction to be issued in IGA v.
Younger. Accordingly, the previous response to your opinion
request No. 75-176 will be and, by this letter, is withdrawn.

(2) The Commission, through appropriate proceedings,
will seek a prompt resolution of the issues raised in IGA v.
Younger relative to the meaning, under the Political Reform
Act, of arranging a contribution.

(3) Pending a definitive ruling in IGA v. Younger, the
Commission will refrain from disapproving the proposal outlined
in your opinion request No. 75-176 and will take no enforcement
action which would be inconsistent with the preliminary injunc-
tion to be issued in IGA based on the Memorandum of Decision of
November 7, 1975. Accordingly, to the extent that the proposed
amendments to the Federation's Constitution authorize you to
communicate with members of the Federation, including making
recommendations concerning campaign contributions, the Commission
will not institute enforcement proceedings based on such
conduct during the pendency of IGA. I should caution you,
however, that the decision in IGA is limited and does not remove
the Act's prohibition against arranging a contribution.

Mr. John Henning
December 2, 1975
Page Two

Specifically, the decision states:

The assumed factual premise is that the lobbyist
has done nothing more than recommend the making
of a contribution. It is assumed that he has not
discussed it with the legislator or his campaign
committee, delivered it, made the decision to give
it, raised the money from which it is made, or
in any way had anything else to do with the con-
tribution. He has merely recommended the making
of the contribution.

IGA v. Younger, at p. 5

(4) Subsequent to a decision in IGA v. Younger, the
Commission will reconsider its opinion whether the proposed
amendments to the Federal Election's Constitution satisfy the requirements of the Political Reform Act. However, before a decision
is made, you will be notified and given an opportunity to be
heard and no enforcement action will be undertaken until after
a new response to opinion request No. 75-176 is adopted.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the Commission

DHL:pvp

.D&N

EDMUND G. BROW4 JR., Governor

.AIA
POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
St-ott
rioma 95814
(916) 322-5660

October 27, 1975

Mr. John F. Henning
Executive Secretary-Treasurer
California Labor Federation
995 Market Street
San Francisco, California 94103

Re: Request For Opinion
No. 75-176

Dear Mr. Henning:

Thank you for your letter of September 16, 1975, re-
questing an opinion from the Fair Political Practices Com-
mission on the following two questions:

1. Do the rule: regulating the making of political
contributions by the Federation's COPE committee,
as stated in the proposed amendment to the
Federation's constitution, meet the requirements
of Government Code §86202?

2. More particularly, are the roles authorized for
the Secretary-Treasurer and Director of the
Federation's COPE committee, who also is a
registered lobbyist, and the roles authorized
for Mr. Gruhn, the President, and for M~. Finks,
the Vice President and a full-time employee, both
of whom also have registered as lobbyists, consist-
ent with the requirements of Government Code §86202?

No formal opinion will be issued by the Commission be-
cause the questions presented do not raise a substantial
question involving an interpretation of the Political Reform
Act. However, the Commission did consider this matter in
public session at its meeting on October 23, 1975, and
authorized the following response.

John F. Henning                                    Page Two


     On June 18, 1975, the Fair Political Practices Commission adopted an opinion in response to your opinion request of January 3, 1975, Opinion, California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO, 1 FPPC Opinions 28 (75-024, June 18, 1975), setting forth the following conclusion:

     Nothing in the Political Reform Act prevents the Federation from continuing to participate fully in the political process. This is so whether members of the staff, members of the executive council or officers of the Federation act as lobbyists. However, those individuals who are lobbyists may not participate in any activities of the Federation which come within the prohibitions of Government Code §86202 and may not serve on COPE, the Federation's political arm.

     A few weeks later, on July 3, 1975, the Commission issued an opinion in response to a request by Donald Green, a registered lobbyist. Opinion, Donald Green, 1 FPPC Opinions 86 (75-098, July 3, 1975). The conclusion in that opinion was that of:

     By advising his or her employer with regard to making political campaign contributions to State officials, the lobbyist has arranged for the making of a contribution as prohibited by §86202 if all of the following criteria are met:

     (1)  the lobbyist communicated with the employer;

     (2)  the advice was given wholly or partially with the intent of influencing the employer's decision to make a campaign contribution;

     (3)  the employer in fact made a contribution; and

     (4)  the lobbyist's advice was a causal element in the making of the contribution.

     The dissemination of factual information concerning a public official's voting record does not fall within the prohibitions of Sections §6200, et seq.

From F. Henr[?]                                                  Page Three

     Reading these two opinions together, it is clear
that your proposed amendments to the Federation's
constitution do not comply with the requirements of
the Political Reform Act. Article XV, Section F, as
amended by adding Section 1, allows the lobbyists of
the Federation "to have voice but no vote in the
political recommendation and endorsement process
relating to candidates for state and local offices..."
In 1 FPPC Opinions 28 (75-004), we state at pages 33-34:

     Nor do we believe lobbyists may participate
in procedures whereby the Federation
endorses candidates for state office (or for
non-state office if the candidate is a state
official). As we understand the procedures
of the Federation, an endorsement by the
Convention is by rule or by practice a
prerequisite to receiving a cash contribution,
and a significant percentage of the candidates
who are endorsed receive cash contributions.
All endorsed candidates are supported in
literature published and distributed by and
at the expense of the Federation. Under
these circumstances, we believe the lobbyist's
participation in the endorsement is closely
enough related to the ultimate contribution
that he may be said to have "arranged" the
contribution within the meaning of §86202.
Any contrary conclusion would undermine the
purposes of §86202 by forcing candidates
and officeholders who hope to receive
contributions from the Federation to curry
the favor of the lobbyist in order to obtain
the endorsement which is a first essential
step toward receiving the contribution.
In this manner the personal influence of
the lobbyist which is traceable to control
of money would be enhanced, in precisely
the manner in which the Political Reform
Act is intended to prohibit.

     It appears obvious that the Commission opinion pro-
hibits your lobbyists from certain types of participation
in the endorsement process. Clearly, this means that the
lobbyist may not vote on an endorsement, and your proposed
amendment contains such a prohibition. The Green opinion,
however, provides guidance as to the other activities in

John F. Henning                                                Page Four

which a lobbyist may or may not engage with respect to
the endorsement. The lobbyist may not, for instance,
make recommendations to the convention as to whom it
should endorse since this could constitute arranging
for a contribution. He may present factual information
which is readily available to members of the public,
may communicate information to the convention concerning
the voting and legislative records of public officials,
and may describe positions taken by various officials
on current issues. This type of activity is an essential
and necessary part of the lobbyist's job. Accordingly,
if the word "voice" meant providing only factual infor-
mation on voting records, this would be permitted by
the Act. However, since the word "voice" is used in
the proposed amendment, is not necessarily limited to
providing factual information and could include activities
which would constitute "arranging" for a contribution,
the amendment does not satisfy the requirements of
Government Code §86202.

    Turning to Article XIX, Section 2 (contributions by
COPE), this proposed amendment to the Federation's con-
stitution prohibits the lobbyist from making recommenda-
tions as to the amounts to be contributed to state and
local candidates but allows the lobbyist to provide such
"advice and information" as the committee requests,
presumably including advice and information relative
to whether or not the candidate should receive a
campaign contribution. The above analysis regarding
Article XV, Section F also is applicable to this
proposed amendment. The proposed amendment still
permits the lobbyist to do more than provide factual
information, and thus permits him to engage in activities
which can constitute arranging for a contribution. This
is particularly true with respect to the Director, who
sits as an ex-officio member of the Subcommittee charged
with the responsibility for making contributions and has
"voice but no vote." Accordingly, this amendment also
does not satisfy the requirements of Government Code
§86202.

    Finally, we observe that the proposed amendments
to the constitution do not respond to the decision of
the Commission that in light of the functions performed
by COPE and the role of the Director and the Chairman
in accomplishing these functions, a lobbyist should not
also be the Director or the Chairman of COPE. As was
observed in the Federation opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 28, 35

Mr. John F. Hunting                                                Page Five


(75-004, June 13, 1975), the Director and the Chairman have
many powers and responsibilities which go beyond merely
directing who gets campaign contributions.

     The Director and Chairman are responsible for
the management and supervision of COPE, and
we do not believe it is realistic to expect
them to divorce themselves entirely from a
major part of COPE's activities. Presumably,
they are responsible for the hiring and firing
of COPE staff. Even if they delegate decisions
regarding contributions to members of the staff,
it is almost inevitable that the staff will
respond to the actual or perceived desires of
the Director and Chairman. Furthermore, the
state official who receives a contribution
from COPE is likely to assume, rightly or
wrongly, that the contribution was ordered by
the Director or the Chairman, thus enhancing
their influence as lobbyists in precisely the
manner that the Act seeks to prevent. For these
reasons, we find that the Secretary-Treasurer
and the President of the Federation cannot
continue to act both as lobbyists and hold these
positions in COPE.

     In your letter of October 1, 1975, you indicate
that it is the intent of the amendments that the COPE staff
which has responsibilities relative to political contribu-
tions will be responsible only to the vice presidents on
the subcommittee set up pursuant to the amendment to
Article XIX. However, as we noted earlier, pursuant to
the proposed amendment, the Director will be an ex-officio
member of the Subcommittee with "voice but no vote." Thus,
the same potential problems identified in the foregoing
quotation from our earlier opinion still will exist. In
other words, it is not clear from the proposed amendments
what intent to make the staff responsible "only to [the]
committee" will be accomplished.

     For the foregoing reasons, it is our view that the
proposed amendments to the constitution do not satisfy
the requirements of the Political Reform Act.

John F. Henning                                                                    Page Six


     If you have any further questions concerning this
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]
                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman


DHL:glb

MEMORANDUM

TO: Carol Brosnahan
Richard J. Carpenter
Anthony I. Miller
Carol Arth Waters
Daniel Lowenstein

FROM: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Kenneth Finney

Subject: Request for Opinion No. 75-176

Attached please find a proposed response to the September 16,
1975, opinion request of John Henning. A brief history
concerning this proposed response may be helpful. After
receipt of Mr. Henning's letter of September 16, 1975, a
proposed response was prepared and circulated among the
Commissioners. The Commission considered the response sat-
isfactory and decided to offer Mr. Henning an opportunity
to present his views relative to the proposed response at
the October 2, 1975, Commission meeting. Mr. Henning was
unable to attend the meeting but sent me a letter express-
ing his views about the proposed response. After considera-
tion of Mr. Henning's letter, a revised proposed response
was prepared, and it is that revised proposed response
which I am forwarding to you today.

In order to facilitate your review of the proposed response,
I also am enclosing all of the papers relevant to this
matter. This includes a copy of the Commission's original
opinion (No. 75-004); the subsequent September 16, 1975,
opinion request from Mr. Henning; a copy of our original
proposed response; and the October 1, 1975, letter from
Mr. Henning expressing his views on that proposed response.

I also should observe that we have received indications
that this matter might result in litigation, and I there-
fore suggest that you review the proposed response with
that possibility in mind. I anticipate consideration of
this matter during the afternoon session of the October 23,
1975, meeting.

James E. McDoughlin

Risley C. Ewing
[illegible]
H. B. Shafer
P. T. S. Maker

555 MARKET STREET, SUITE 310
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94103
415 392-3525

October 1, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Mr. Kenneth Finney
            Director, Legal Division

Re: Request for Opinion No. 75-176
     California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO

Dear Mr. Finney:

     This will acknowledge your letter of September 26, 1975, addressed to me and attached draft dated September 26, 1975, addressed to me by Chairman Daniel H. Lowenstein of the Fair Political Practices Commission in response to our request for a ruling contained in my letter of September 16, 1975, addressed to you.

     With respect to the invitation to appear at the meeting of the Commission on Thursday, October 2, 1975, when the draft will be considered, I regret that it will be impossible for me or any representative of the Federation to be present since the AFL-CIO is meeting in National Convention in San Francisco and I and all of our officers and staff are tied up with a multiplicity of obligations.

     I do wish, however, to express my views in writing by this commission and hops that they will be considered by the Commission.

     On Page 1 of the draft, the last paragraph, it is stated

     "On June 18, 1975, the Fair Political Practices Commission issued an opinion in response to your request of January 3, 1975, FPPC Opinion 75-004..."

- 2 -

While the Commission may have voted on the request on that
date, we received no opinion, other than drafts, until we
received a letter dated August 25, 1975, on August 28, 1975,
from Jeanne Pritchard, Consultant, Fair Political Practices
Commission, transmitting the final copy of the FPPC Opinion
No. 75-004.

On Page 3 of your letter, you characterize the good faith
efforts of the Federation to comply with the letter and
spirit of the law as "your proposed amendment reflects an
acceptance of that prohibition." We believe you have
totally mischaracterized the good faith efforts of the
Federation to comply with the letter and spirit of the
law as you misinterpret it.

The thrust of the portion of Page 4 of the draft beginning
with the phrase "Finally, we observe (etc.)" is that the
proposed amendments to the Federation's constitution vest
the authority to make "contributions" as that term is defined
in Section 86202 in individuals who are under the control of
registered lobbyists. That reading of the proposal is plainly
wrong. The proposal places the entire authority to make such
contributions in a committee of three individually elected
Vice Presidents. As to matters within the committee's purview,
the intent is that the COPE staff will be responsible only to
that committee. We note this point out of an abundance of
caution for your draft on its face refers to the stopgap
delegation of authority to the Assistant Director of COPE,
Mr. Callahan, (which does not permit the making of contributions)
and which is in effect pending final resolution of the issues
raised by the Federation's request for ruling.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

John F. Henning
Executive Secretary-Treasurer

PRACTICES COMMISSION

September 26, 1975

Mr. John F. Henning
Executive Secretary-Treasurer
California Labor Federation
995 Market Street
San Francisco, California 94103

Dear Mr. Henning:

Pursuant to our telephone conversation of
today, I am forwarding to you a copy of the staff's
proposed response to your request for an opinion.
This proposed response will be presented to the
Commission at its meeting on October 2 and, as I
indicated on the telephone, you will be given an
opportunity to express your views concerning the
proposed response if you wish to do so.

Very truly yours,

Kenneth Finney
Director, Legal Division

Enc.

Mr. John F. Henning
Executive Secretary-Treasurer
California Labor Federation
995 Market Street
San Francisco, California 94103

Dear Mr. Henning:

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-176

Thank you for your letter of September 16, 1975,
requesting an opinion from the Fair Political Practices
Commission on the following two questions:

1. Do the rules regulating the making of political
contributions by the Federation's COPE committee, as stated
in the proposed amendment to the Federation's constitution,
meet the requirements of Government Code Section 86202?

2. More particularly, are the roles authorized for
the Secretary-Treasurer and Director of the Federation's
COPE committee, who also is a registered lobbyist, and the
roles authorized for Mr. Gruhn, the President, and for
Mr. Finks, the Vice President and a full time employee,
both of whom also have registered as lobbyists, consistent
with the requirements of Government Code Section 86202?

No formal opinion will be issued by the Commission
because the questions presented do not raise a substantial
question involving an interpretation of the Political
Reform Act. The following analysis will explain the reasons
for the Commission's action.

On June 18, 1975, the Fair Political Practices Commission
issued an opinion in response to your opinion request of
January 3, 1975, FPPC Opinion 75-004, setting forth the
following conclusion:

"Nothing in the Political Reform Act prevents
the Federation from continuing to participate
fully in the political process. This is so
whether members of the staff, members of the
executive council or officers of the Federation
act as lobbyists. However, those individuals
who are lobbyists may not participate in any
activities of the Federation which come within
the prohibitions of Government Code Section
86202 and may not serve on COPE, the Federation's
political arm."

C. John F. Henning
Page Two.                                September 25, 1975


     A few weeks later, on July 3, 1975, the Commission issued
FPPC Opinion 75-098 requested by Donald Craton, a registered
lobbyist. The conclusion in that opinion was that:

     "By advising his or her employer with regard to
     making political campaign contributions to state
     officials, the lobbyist has arranged for the
     making of a contribution as prohibited by
     Section 86202 if all of the following criteria
     are met:

     (1)  the lobbyist communicated with the employer;

     (2)  the advice was given wholly or partially
          with the intent of influencing the employer's
          decision to make a campaign contribution;

     (3)  the employer in fact made a contribution; and

     (4)  the lobbyist's advice was a causal element in
          the making of the contribution.

     The dissemination of factual information concerning
     a public official's voting record does not fall
     within the prohibitions of Sections 86200, et seg."

     Reading these two opinions together, it would appear clear
that your proposed amendments to the Federation's constitution
do not comply with the requirements of the Political Reform
Act. Article XV, Section 1, as amended by adding Section 1,
allows the lobbyists of the Federation "to have voice but no
vote in the political recommendation and endorsement process
relating to candidates for state and local offices. . . ."
FPPC Opinion 75-004 states on page 6:

     "Nor do we believe lobbyists may participate in
     the procedures whereby the Federation endorses
     candidates for state office (or for non-state
     office if the candidate is a state official).
     As we understand the procedures of the Federation,
     an endorsement by the Convention is by rule or by
     practice a pre-requisite to receiving a cash con-
     tribution, and a significant percentage of the
     candidates who are endorsed receive cash contribu-
     tions. All endorsed candidates are supported in
     literature published and distributed by and at the
     expense of the Federation. Under these circumstances,

C. John F. Henning
Page Three                                September 26, 1975


     we believe the lobbyist's participation in the
endorsement is closely enough related to the
ultimate contribution that he may be said to
have "arranged" the contribution within the
meaning of Section 86202. Any contrary conclusion would undermine the purposes of Section
86202 by forcing candidates and officeholders
who hope to receive contributions from the
Federation to curry the favor of the lobbyist
in order to obtain the endorsements which is
a first essential step towards receiving the
contribution. In this manner the personal
influence of the lobbyist which is traceable
to control of money would be enhanced, in
precisely the manner in which the Political
Reform Act is intended to prohibit."

     It appears obvious that the Commission opinion prohibits
your proposed amendment completely. Clearly, this means that the lobbyist may not vote at the
endorsement, and your proposed amendment reflects an acceptance
of that prohibition. The Green opinion, however, provides
guidance as to other activities in which a lobbyist may
or may not engage with respect to the endorsement. The
lobbyist may not, for instance, make recommendations to the
convention as to whom it should endorse since this could
constitute arranging for a contribution. He may present
factual information which is readily available to members of
the public and may communicate information to the convention
concerning the voting and legislative record of the public
official; he may relate factual information concerning bills
that affect the Federation's interest; and he may describe
positions taken by various officials on current issues. This
type of activity is an essential and necessary part of the
lobbyist's job. Accordingly, if the word "voice" mean providing only factual information on voting records, this would
be permitted by the Act. However, since the word "voice," as
used in the proposed amendment, is not necessarily limited to
providing only factual information and could include activities
which would constitute "arranging" for a contribution, the
amendment does not satisfy the requirements of Government Code
Section 86202.

     Turning to Article XIX, Section 2 (contributions by COPE),
this proposed amendment to the Federation's constitution prohibits the lobbyist from making recommendations as to the amounts

Mr. John F. Henning
Page Four                                    September 26, 1975


to be contributed to state and local candidates but allows
the lobbyist to provide such advice and information as the
committee requests, presumably including advice and informa-
tion relative to whether or not the candidate should receive
a campaign contribution. The above analysis regarding
Article XV, Section F also is applicable to this proposed
amendment. The proposed amendments still permits the
lobbyist to do more than provide factual information.
Accordingly, this amendment also does not satisfy the
requirements of Government Code Section 86202.

     Finally, we observe that the proposed amendments to the
constitution do not respond to the decision of the Commis-
sion that the Director of COPP also should not be a lobbyist.
As was observed in the Federation opinion, the Director has
many powers and responsibilities which go beyond merely
directing who gets campaign contributions and therefore:

          "Even if they [the Director and Chairman]
          delegate decisions regarding contributions
          to members of the staff, it is almost
          inevitable that the staff will respond to
          the actual or perceived desire of the
          Director and Chairman."

     Accordingly, although the Director has, by letter, designated
Mr. Callahan to be responsible for receiving contributions
and making disbursements, it seems clear that the Director
still is charged with the responsibility of supervising
Mr. Callahan, and perhaps may even be able to fire him.
Delegating certain responsibilities to Mr. Callahan there-
fore does not satisfy the Commission's decision that the
Director also should not be a lobbyist.

     For the foregoing reasons, it is our view that the pro-
posed amendments to the constitution do not satisfy the
requirements of the Political Reform Act.

     If you have any further questions concerning this
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman

Lloyd J. Lea

VICE PRESIDENTS

[illegible]
[illegible]

[illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]

695 MARKET STREET, SUITE 312
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94103
415-543-3355

September 16, 1975

Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Re: Political Reform Act of 1974
     Application for Ruling by
     California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

The California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO, hereby requests
the Commission to answer the following questions:

1. Do the rules regulating the making of political
contributions by the Federation's COPE Committee as stated
in the Federation Constitution, as proposed to be amended,
meet the requirements of Government Code Section 86202?

2. Most particularly, is the role permitted Secretary-
Treasurer and Director of the Federation's COPE Committee,
who is a registered lobbyist, and the role of Mr. Gruhn,
the President, and Mr. Pinks, Vice President and full
time employee, both of whom have also registered, consistent
with the requirements of Government Code Section 86202?

THE BACKGROUND

1. On August 26, 1975, the Commission issued Opinion
No. 75-004 dealing with earlier questions posed by the
Federation. In the course of that procedure, the
Federation presented evidence as to the present course
it then followed in the making of political contributions
within the meaning of the Act. We request the record so
made be incorporated herein.

2. On June 13, 1975, AFL-CIO President George Meany
stated his approval of proposed amendments to the
California Federation's Constitution designed to bring
Federation's activities into conformity with the Act.
The Commission did not address itself to the changes in
its Opinion No. 75-004.

3. In letters dated August 22 and August 27, 1975
(copies attached) in respect to the draft and final opinions
in 75-004, and on the advice of Counsel, the Federation's
two chief elected officers, both of whom are registered
lobbyists within the meaning of the Act, delegated to
Federation Vice President and Assistant COPE Director
M. R. Callahan, their authority with regard to the receipt
and disbursements of monies utilized for the making of
political contributions to state and local candidates
within the meaning of the Act, subject to the condition
that Mr. Callahan was to make only such disbursements as
in his independent judgment are necessary to meet COPE
Committee's operating expenses and that he was not to
make any political contributions to candidates for state
and local office. (In addition, Vice President Finks,
also a registered lobbyist within the meaning of the Act,
in a letter of August 28, 1975 (copy attached) to
Mr. Callahan and also on the advice of Counsel, withdrew
from an active role in the Federation's COPE Committee
insofar as the latter receives and disburses monies
utilized for the making of political contributions.)

The above-noted condition was imposed because it was
believed it would then be improper for Federation
officers to permit the making of political contributions
in any manner other than as authorized by Federation
members in Convention action.

Also attached are copies of letters from the Federation's
two chief elected officers dated September 12 and September
16, 1975 directed to Federation Vice President and Assistant
COPE Director M. R. Callahan with respect to their functions.

The result is that Opinion 75-004 has brought
Federation activities with respect to state and local
elections to a standstill.

4. On September 8, 1975, AFL-CIO President George
Meany stated approval of more far reaching amendments to
the California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO, Constitution
designed to bring its activities into conformity with
the Act. A copy of that letter is attached. The
Federation's Secretary-Treasurer will present

Daniel H. Lowenstein                -3-                                    9/16/75


the proposed amendments to the Executive Council at its
scheduled meeting of September 17-18, 1975 for the
purpose of securing endorsement of the proposed amendments.
After that step has been taken, the approval could be
ratified at the next regular convention of the Federation.

     5. In light of the paralyzing effect of Opinion 75-004
on the Federation's political activities and since the
Federation has taken every reasonable step to amend the
rules governing its political activities in order to
bring those activities into conformity with the Act, it
is urgently requested that the Commission answer the
above-noted questions expeditiously.

     These are the second set of questions propounded by the
California Labor Federation. The Federation's legal
position was fully stated in connection with its earlier
inquiries. Therefore, I do not attach a legal memorandum
to this request. Should the Commission believe that such
a memorandum would be of assistance, the Federation will
file one promptly upon being so advised.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        John F. Henning
                                        Executive Secretary-Treasurer

JFH:ka
ope 3-afl-cio (31)
Enclosures

cc: Carol Waters, Commissioner, FPPC
    Carol Brosnahan, Commissioner, FPPC
    Anthony Miller, Commissioner, FPPC
    Richard Carpenter, Commissioner, FPPC
    Robert Stern, General Counsel, FPPC
    Albin J. Gruhn
    Harry Finks
    Larry Gold, Esquire
    George Meany
    J. Albert Woll, Esquire
    C. P. Scully, Esquire

AMERICAN FEDERATION OF LABOR AND CONGRESS OF INDUSTRIAL ORGANIZATION

GEORGE MEANY LANE KIRKLAND
PRESIDENT SECRETARY-TREASURER

815 SIXTEENTH STREET, N.W.
WASHINGTON, D.C.
(202) 637-5000

September 8, 1975

Mr. John F. Henning, Executive
Secretary-Treasurer
California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO
995 Market Street, Suite 310
San Francisco, California 94103

Dear Sir and Brother:

In response to your request, this is to advise you that
acting pursuant to my authority to interpret the Rules Governing
AFL-CIO State Central Bodies, and to approve the Constitution and
Bylaws of such bodies, I have determined that should the California
Labor Federation, AFL-CIO, wish to consider and propose the following amendments to its Constitution in an effort to bring itself
into compliance with the State's so-called Political Reform Act
as interpreted by the State's Fair Political Practices Commission,
such a course would be proper, and such amendments, if adopted,
would be approved:

Amend Article XV, Section F by adding a new Section 1
reading:

Officers of the Federation, members of the Executive Council
and delegates to a Special Pre-Primary Election Convention and/or
a Pre-General Election Convention who are "prohibited" within the
meaning of the State of California Political Reform Act of 1974
shall have voice but no vote in the political recommendation and
endorsement process relating to candidates for State and Local
offices outlined in this Article.

Amend Article XIX by renumbering Section 2 Section 3, and by
adding the following new Section 2:

embedding.qa_text: December 3, 1975 Mr. John F. Henning Executive Secretary-Treasurer California Labor Federation 995 Market Street San Francisco, Ca. 94103 Re: Opinion Request No. 75-176 Dear Mr. Henning: At its meeting on December 2, 1975, the Commission considered its previous response to your request for an opinion, No. 75-176, in light of the decision by the Los Angeles County Superior Court in Institute of Governmental Advocates v. Younger, No. C 110,052, and authorized me to advise you of the following: (1) Although the Commission continues to believe that the views expressed in my letter of October 27, 1975, represent a correct interpretation of the law, the Commission intends to comply with the preliminary injunction to be issued in IGA v. Younger. Accordingly, the previous response to your opinion request No. 75-176 will be and, by this letter, is withdrawn. (2) The Commission, through appropriate proceedings, will seek a prompt resolution of the issues raised in IGA v. Younger relative to the meaning, under the Political Reform Act, of arranging a contribution. (3) Pending a definitive ruling in IGA v. Younger, the Commission will refrain from disapproving the proposal outlined in your opinion request No. 75-176 and will take no enforcement action which would be inconsistent with the preliminary injunction to be issued in IGA based on the Memorandum of Decision of November 7, 1975. Accordingly, to the extent that the proposed amendments to the Federation's Constitution authorize you to communicate with members of the Federation, including making recommendations concerning campaign contributions, the Commission will not institute enforcement proceedings based on such conduct during the pendency of IGA. I should caution you, however, that the decision in IGA is limited and does not remove the Act's prohibition against arranging a contribution. Mr. John Henning December 2, 1975 Page Two Specifically, the decision states: The assumed factual premise is that the lobbyist has done nothing more than recommend the making of a contribution. It is assumed that he has not discussed it with the legislator or his campaign committee, delivered it, made the decision to give it, raised the money from which it is made, or in any way had anything else to do with the contribution. He has merely recommended the making of the contribution. IGA v. Younger, at p. 5 (4) Subsequent to a decision in IGA v. Younger, the Commission will reconsider its opinion whether the proposed amendments to the Federation's Constitution satisfy the requirements of the Political Reform Act. However, before a decision is made, you will be notified and given an opportunity to be heard and no enforcement action will be undertaken until after a new response to opinion request No. 75-176 is adopted. Very truly yours, [Signature] Daniel H. Lowenstein Chairman, for the Commission October 27, 1975 Mr. John F. Henning Executive Secretary-Treasurer California Labor Federation 995 Market Street San Francisco, California 94103 Re: Request For Opinion No. 75-176 Dear Mr. Henning: Thank you for your letter of September 16, 1975, requesting an opinion from the Fair
